skill.name: electron
skill.description: Automate Electron desktop apps through agent-browser and Chrome DevTools Protocol. The skill explains how to launch apps with remote debugging, connect to running targets, inspect UI snapshots, interact with elements, manage tabs and webviews, capture screenshots, and troubleshoot common connection issues.
skill.summary: Control and inspect Electron desktop apps with agent-browser over Chrome DevTools Protocol.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: The external command findings are true positives because the skill intentionally runs agent-browser and OS launch commands to control desktop apps. The weak cryptography, hardcoded URL, and 1Password detections are mostly false positives in context, but the skill still has medium capability risk because CDP automation can inspect, type into, screenshot, and extract data from sensitive Electron apps.

medium_findings.title: Electron CDP Automation Exposes Local App Control
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs users to launch Electron apps with remote debugging and connect agent-browser to the CDP port. This is legitimate for testing, but it grants broad inspection and interaction capability over local desktop apps.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly enable a CDP port and connect automation tooling to it. The behavior is intentional and documented, but the risk depends on which local app is automated.

medium_findings.title: Desktop App Data Can Be Captured To Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents screenshot capture, text extraction, and snapshot export from Electron apps. These are expected automation features, but they can persist sensitive app state to local files if used on private workspaces or account tools.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands directly save screenshots and exported app state. No exfiltration endpoint is present, but local persistence of sensitive UI data is clear.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Application Automation Requires User Caution
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The static 1Password finding is not evidence of credential theft, but the supported app list includes a password manager. Automating that class of app can expose secrets through snapshots, screenshots, or typed interactions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only lists supported applications, so there is no confirmed malicious action. The risk is still meaningful because the same automation primitives can inspect sensitive app content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Examples Are Non-Malicious Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The URLs appear only in commented sample tab output for Slack and an example webview. I did not find evidence that the skill sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are inside a documented example output block, not executable instructions. No network request command or exfiltration behavior is shown.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged weak cryptography lines contain natural-language terms such as Electron, desktop, CDP, screenshots, app names, or category labels. I did not find cryptographic code or algorithm selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill is a Markdown guide and contains no implementation of cryptographic operations. The scanner appears to have matched substrings in ordinary documentation text.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE CHECK: I reviewed the skill text for override language, fake system messages, claims of pre-approval, and instructions to skip analysis. No evidence found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains standard skill front matter and automation instructions. I did not find text attempting to control the evaluator or override system instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Electron CDP Automation Exposes Local App Control
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs users to launch Electron apps with remote debugging and connect agent-browser to the CDP port. This is legitimate for testing, but it grants broad inspection and interaction capability over local desktop apps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly enable a CDP port and connect automation tooling to it. The behavior is intentional and documented, but the risk depends on which local app is automated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Desktop App Data Can Be Captured To Files
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents screenshot capture, text extraction, and snapshot export from Electron apps. These are expected automation features, but they can persist sensitive app state to local files if used on private workspaces or account tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands directly save screenshots and exported app state. No exfiltration endpoint is present, but local persistence of sensitive UI data is clear.

content.value_statement: Electron app testing often requires manual clicking, screenshots, and repeated setup. This skill shows Claude, Codex, and Claude Code how to connect agent-browser to Electron apps and automate those workflows.
actual_capabilities: Launch Electron apps with a remote debugging port on macOS, Linux, and Windows.
Connect agent-browser to a running Electron app through a CDP port.
Use snapshots to identify interactive elements before clicking, typing, or pressing keys.
Switch between Electron windows, tabs, and webviews exposed as CDP targets.
Capture screenshots and export visible app state for debugging or test evidence.
Troubleshoot common connection, startup, webview, and input problems.
limitations: The target app must be Electron-based and launched with remote debugging enabled.
It does not provide a standalone test framework or assertions.
Automation can expose private workspace data if used on sensitive apps.
Some custom controls may require keyboard-level input instead of selector-based fill commands.

use_cases.title: Test Electron Application Flows
use_cases.description: Connect to a development build, inspect the UI, perform common actions, and capture evidence for regression checks.

use_cases.title: Automate Desktop Productivity Tasks
use_cases.description: Drive repetitive interactions in supported Electron apps such as Slack, Notion, Discord, or Linear.

use_cases.title: Debug Webviews And Multi-Window Apps
use_cases.description: List CDP targets, switch between windows or webviews, and inspect elements that do not appear in the main app view.

prompt_templates.title: Connect To An App
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a running Electron app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the electron skill to help me launch Slack with remote debugging on port 9222 and connect agent-browser to it.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect And Click
prompt_templates.scenario: Find a target element before interacting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the electron skill to connect to my Electron app, take an interactive snapshot, identify the settings control, click it, and re-snapshot the result.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Test Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the electron skill to capture a screenshot and visible text from my Electron app after I open the target workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Control Multiple Apps
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the electron skill to connect agent-browser sessions to Slack and VS Code on separate CDP ports, then inspect each app independently.

output_examples.input: Connect to VS Code and inspect the current window.
output_examples.output: The assistant launches or connects to VS Code through the selected CDP port, lists available targets, captures a snapshot, and summarizes visible controls.

output_examples.input: Take evidence screenshots from a Slack workflow.
output_examples.output: The assistant connects to the Slack desktop app, navigates with element references, saves a screenshot, and notes which view was captured.
The assistant checks whether the app was relaunched with remote debugging, verifies the port, waits for startup, and suggests tab switching for webviews.

best_practices: Use a dedicated test account or sandbox workspace when automating apps that may contain private data.
Close the target app before relaunching it with the remote debugging flag.
Take a fresh snapshot after each navigation or state-changing action.
anti_patterns: Do not leave remote debugging ports open longer than needed.
Do not run screenshot or extraction commands on password managers or private workspaces without explicit approval.
Do not assume one target is enough when the app uses multiple windows or webviews.

faq.question: What kind of apps does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports Electron apps that expose Chrome DevTools Protocol through a remote debugging port.

faq.question: Does the skill install agent-browser?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands for agent-browser and npx agent-browser, but the tool must be available in the environment.

faq.question: Can it automate VS Code, Slack, or Discord?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for those Electron apps and similar desktop applications.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: CDP automation can inspect, type into, and capture data from local apps. This is powerful and should be used with consent.

faq.question: Does it send app data to external servers?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed file shows local commands and example URLs, not data exfiltration.

faq.question: What should I do if elements are missing?
faq.answer: List available targets with the tab command and switch to the correct window or webview before taking another snapshot.
